conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions. Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child. My son Alex is 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

To continue assessing your eligibility, I need to ask:

**Does Alex live with you, or does he live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand Alex's current situation:

**Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?** (For example, is he studying for A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications at school or college?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's actually just left school recently. He's registered with the careers service to figure out what he wants to do next - whether to go back to college or look for work.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is helpful to know.

**When exactly did Alex leave school?** I need to know the specific date or at least the month and year, as this is important for determining eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He left school at the end of the summer term, so that would have been late June this year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Just to confirm a few more details:

**Has Alex started any paid work since leaving school in June, or is he currently unemployed and registered with the careers service?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's not working at the moment - just registered with the careers service while he figures out his next steps.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you currently living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship to Alex:

**Are you Alex's parent, or do you have parental responsibility for him?** (For example, are you his biological parent, adoptive parent, or legal guardian?)

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just one more important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** (For example, Alex's other parent or another family member?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

Although Alex is 16 and has left school, you qualify for the **20-week extension period** that allows Child Benefit to continue after a young person leaves education or training. This applies because Alex has registered with the careers service.

Since Alex left school in late June (approximately 5-6 months ago), you are still within this 20-week extension window. You also meet all the other eligibility criteria:
- You are Alex's parent with parental responsibility
- Alex lives with you in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**Important to note:** The 20-week extension will eventually end. If Alex then goes back into approved education or training, your Child Benefit can continue. However, if he doesn't return to education or training and the 20-week period expires, your Child Benefit for Alex will stop. 

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, I recommend you apply as soon as possible. If you're already receiving it, it should continue during this extension period.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 16 years old and left school in late June of this year. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can claim Child Benefit for a child aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. Importantly, there is an extension period: Child Benefit continues for 20 weeks after a young person leaves education or training if they register with the careers service or Connexions (in England). Since Alex left school in late June (approximately 5-6 months ago, which is within the 20-week extension period) and is registered with the careers service, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex during this extension period. The claimant is Alex's parent, lives with Alex in the UK (Manchester), is responsible for Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. All eligibility criteria are met during this extension period.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old son Alex. Although Alex has left school, he is registered with the careers service, which means the claimant qualifies for the 20-week extension period that allows Child Benefit to continue after a young person leaves education. Since Alex left school in late June (approximately 5-6 months ago), this falls within the 20-week extension window. The claimant meets all other criteria: they are Alex's parent, Alex lives with them in the UK, and no one else is claiming for Alex.
